frameworks.name: Investment Hierarchy
frameworks.description: Not all investments are equal. Sequence by expected return and control level. Most people skip to Step 4 before maximizing Steps 1-3.
steps: Step 1 — Eliminate high-interest debt: guaranteed return equal to interest rate. No investment reliably beats 20%+ credit card rates.
Step 2 — Invest in yourself: skills and network produce 10-100x returns. Highest expected return of any asset class early in career.
Step 3 — Tax-advantaged accounts: 401K match (100% immediate return), IRA, HSA. Floor investment before anything else.
Step 4 — Business equity: own equity in your own business before speculating in others. You have information advantage.
Step 5 — Index funds: low-cost, diversified, tax-efficient. Market returns without stock-picking risk.
Step 6 — Alternative investments: real estate, angel, private equity. Only after Steps 1-5 are maximized.
antipatterns: Angel investing before maxing tax-advantaged accounts — skipping guaranteed return for speculative
Stock picking without edge — 90%+ of active managers underperform index over 10+ years
Real estate before emergency fund — illiquid investment before liquid safety net

frameworks.name: Compounding Mechanics
frameworks.description: Compounding is not intuitive. Understanding the actual math changes behavior — especially around time horizon and consistency.
steps: Rule of 72: divide 72 by your annual return to find years to double. 7% = 10.3 years. 10% = 7.2 years.
Time dominates return rate: $1,000 at 10% for 30 years = $17,449. Same investment for 40 years = $45,259. 10 extra years = 2.6x.
Consistency dominates timing: $500/month for 30 years at 8% = $754,000. Missing the 10 best days in the market over 30 years reduces return by ~50%.
Cost drag compounds: 1% annual fee on $500K over 20 years = ~$130,000 in lost compounding. Minimize fees ruthlessly.
Tax drag compounds: tax-advantaged accounts preserve compounding that taxable accounts lose annually.
Visualize your specific numbers: calculate actual projected balances at your savings rate — abstraction kills motivation
antipatterns: Interrupting compounding (withdrawals, pauses) — the last years are the most valuable
High-fee funds when low-cost equivalents exist — fee difference compounds into enormous cost over decades
Waiting for 'the right time' to invest — time in market beats timing the market consistently

frameworks.name: Portfolio Architecture
frameworks.description: Portfolio design should reflect your actual risk tolerance (behavioral, not stated), time horizon, and need for liquidity — not a generic allocation.
steps: Define your time horizon: when do you need this money? Under 3 years = capital preservation. 3-10 years = balanced. 10+ years = growth.
Define your behavioral risk tolerance: not 'how much volatility can you theoretically handle' but 'what drawdown would make you sell?'
Core holding for most long-horizon investors: low-cost total market index funds (domestic + international)
Bond allocation as volatility buffer, not return driver: rough rule — your age as bond percentage (adjust for risk tolerance)
Rebalance annually, not on market movements — prevents emotion-driven allocation drift
Caveat: consult a fee-only fiduciary advisor for personalized allocation
antipatterns: Stated risk tolerance ≠ behavioral risk tolerance — know which one you actually have
Overweighting domestic equities — home country bias is a documented behavioral error
Rebalancing on market movements rather than schedule — converts a system into emotional reaction

prompts.name: Investment Hierarchy Audit
prompts.template: Audit my investment hierarchy. High-interest debt: [amount and rates]. 401K match captured: [yes/no]. IRA/HSA maxed: [yes/no]. Business equity owned: [describe]. Current investments: [describe]. Identify where I am in the hierarchy, where I am skipping steps, and the highest-ROI next action.

prompts.name: Compounding Projection
prompts.template: Project my compounding trajectory. Current investable savings: [amount]. Monthly contribution: [amount]. Expected return: [% or use 7% real]. Time horizon: [years]. Show me the Rule of 72 doubling schedule, the projected balance, and the cost of a 5-year delay in starting.

prompts.name: Portfolio Architecture Review
prompts.template: Review my portfolio architecture. Current allocation: [describe]. Time horizon: [years]. Behavioral reaction to 30% drawdown: [would hold / would reduce / would sell]. Fees: [describe]. Identify allocation gaps, fee drag, and recommended rebalancing. Note: I will consult a fee-only advisor before making changes.

examples.title: Early Career Investment Sequencing
examples.context: 28-year-old with $15K in high-interest debt, no 401K, $500/month available
examples.correct_sequence: 1) Eliminate $15K debt (guaranteed 18% return). 2) Capture 401K match (100% return). 3) Build $10K emergency fund. 4) Max IRA. 5) Additional index fund investment.
examples.incorrect_sequence_avoided: Investing $500/month in stocks while carrying 18% debt — guaranteed negative arbitrage

vocabulary.definition: Divide 72 by annual return rate to estimate years required to double an investment. Quick compounding calculator.
A fund tracking a market index (e.g., S&P 500). Provides diversification at minimal cost. Outperforms most active management over long horizons.
The compounding cost of investment fees over time. 1% annual fee compounds to enormous cost over 20-30 year horizons.
Investment accounts with tax benefits: 401K, IRA, HSA, 529. Preserves compounding that would otherwise be lost to annual taxation.
Periodically restoring portfolio to target allocation by selling outperformers and buying underperformers. Enforces buy-low-sell-high discipline.
Financial advisor legally required to act in client's best interest. Distinct from broker operating under lesser suitability standard.
The actual drawdown percentage at which an investor will sell, as opposed to stated tolerance. The binding constraint in real market conditions.
Documented behavioral tendency to overweight domestic equities. Reduces diversification without improving expected returns.

sources.title: The Little Book of Common Sense Investing
sources.relevance: Index fund case, fee drag, long-term compounding

sources.title: The Psychology of Money
sources.relevance: Behavioral finance, compounding patience, wealth building

Constraint Mapper (behavioral vs stated risk tolerance)
master_laws: Law 19 (Compound — investment is compound interest made tangible)
Law 18 (Reality — market returns are not controllable)
integration_hooks.caveat: Strategic framework only. Not financial advice. Consult a fee-only fiduciary advisor for personalized investment decisions.

4_gate_validation.safe: PASS — caveat embedded; no specific securities recommendations
4_gate_validation.true: PASS — index fund superiority and compounding mechanics are well-established
4_gate_validation.high_leverage: PASS — investment sequencing hierarchy prevents common high-cost errors
